topic: A person discovers their mentor has retired without saying goodbye
raw_output:
[story 1]

The office felt strangely hollow. Sunlight streamed across the mahogany desk where Elias used to leave his meticulously organized notes, but the silence was the loudest thing in the room. I kept glancing at the empty chair across from me, a familiar, tight knot forming in my stomach. He was supposed to be here. His usual booming laugh, the precise way he’d correct my syntax—gone. I ran a hand over my jaw, the skin feeling suddenly too tight. I started tidying his workspace, straightening a stack of papers that didn't need straightening, a compulsive need to impose order on the sudden, unsettling void. When I finally looked up, expecting to see him leaning against the doorframe, the door was closed, and the silence remained absolute.

[story 2]

I checked the schedule three times. Every time, the slot designated for Dr. Alistair’s weekly consultation was marked as vacant. A cold prickle traced down my spine, making the hairs on my arms stand up. I paced the length of the corridor, my footsteps echoing unnervingly on the polished floor. My palms were slick against the cool metal of the water cooler. I tried to focus on the data on my screen, the numbers blurring into meaningless streaks. Why hadn't he called? Why was there no note? A tremor ran through my hands as I gripped the edge of my desk, fighting the urge to check his personal phone, a purely irrational impulse fueled by a deep-seated anxiety that something fundamental had shifted in our professional landscape.

[story 3]

The news had filtered through the department like a slow, unwelcome tide. Retirement. It was a fact, delivered with the same flat indifference he usually reserved for administrative updates. But the absence was a gaping wound. I watched the receptionist with an intensity that made my gaze feel heavy, cataloging every slight shift in her posture, every pause before she answered the phone. My breathing became shallow, quick catches in my chest. I found myself re-reading old correspondence, tracing the faded ink of past collaborations, searching for a clue, a subtle hint that he hadn't simply vanished into the ether. Each unanswered query felt like another layer of uncertainty settling over me, demanding constant, exhausting attention.
